Cleopatra lived closer in time to the moon landing than to the building of the Great Pyramid of Giza.
Oxford University was teaching students centuries before the Aztec Empire existed. Tenochtitlan was only founded in 1325.
Woolly mammoths still roamed Wrangel Island while the pyramids of Giza were being built in Egypt.
Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company, back when the Ottoman Empire still ruled and Queen Victoria sat on the throne.
A fax machine was patented in 1843, nearly two decades before the American Civil War began.
Harvard University was founded in 1636, decades before calculus was invented.
Roman concrete gets stronger with age. Seawater reacts with its minerals, which is why ancient harbors still stand.
Despite the name, the Hundred Years War between England and France actually lasted 116 years.
History's shortest war was fought between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896. It lasted around 40 minutes.
When Britain adopted the Gregorian calendar in 1752, eleven days simply vanished. September 2 was followed directly by September 14.
Sweden once had a February 30. It appeared exactly once, in the year 1712, to fix a calendar mistake.
Paris nearly lost the Eiffel Tower. It went up as a temporary exhibit with a 20 year permit, saved partly because it made a superb radio mast.
Anne Frank, Martin Luther King Junior, and Barbara Walters were all born in the same year, 1929.
Charlie Chaplin and Adolf Hitler were born just four days apart in April 1889.
France last used the guillotine in 1977, the same year Star Wars premiered in cinemas.
Japan's last great samurai rebellion happened in 1877, one year after the telephone was patented.
For over 3,800 years the Great Pyramid was the tallest structure on Earth, until an English cathedral finally passed it around 1311.
Ancient Rome reached a million residents. No European city matched that again until London around the year 1800.
There was no single fire that destroyed the Library of Alexandria. It faded away over centuries of neglect and shrinking funding.
Vikings reached North America around the year 1000, nearly five centuries before Columbus set sail.
Millvina Dean was two months old when she survived the Titanic. She lived until 2009, the last survivor of the sinking.
President John Tyler was born in 1790, yet he still had a living grandson in the 2020s. Two generations spanned 230 years.
A university in Fez, Morocco has been teaching since the year 859, making it the oldest continuously operating university on Earth.
Ancient Greece held Olympic games for nearly twelve centuries straight, from 776 BC until a Roman emperor shut them down.
Wealthy Romans bought gladiator sweat, scraped off with a blade and bottled, to use as a cosmetic.
Aztecs used cacao beans as money. You could literally spend chocolate at the market.
When the Spanish arrived, the Aztec capital Tenochtitlan held around 200,000 people, larger than almost any city in Europe.
Without wheels, iron tools, or horses, the Inca built about 40,000 kilometers of roads through the Andes.
Machu Picchu was under construction around the same time Gutenberg was printing his famous Bible in Germany.
The Mongol Empire remains the largest contiguous land empire in history, stretching from Korea to Hungary.
Nobody knows where Genghis Khan is buried. His grave has stayed hidden for nearly 800 years.
Typhoons wrecked two Mongol invasion fleets bound for Japan. The Japanese called those storms kamikaze, the divine wind.
The Black Death killed somewhere between 30 and 60 percent of Europe's entire population in just a few years.
Isaac Newton ran England's Royal Mint and went undercover in taverns to catch counterfeiters, several of whom were hanged.
Israel offered Albert Einstein its presidency in 1952. He turned it down, saying he lacked the aptitude for dealing with people.
In 1932 Australia sent soldiers with machine guns against emus. The birds won, and the episode is remembered as the Great Emu War.
A dancing plague struck Strasbourg in 1518. Dozens of people danced uncontrollably for days on end.
A ruptured vat sent over a million liters of beer flooding through a London neighborhood in 1814, killing eight people.
In 1919 a wave of molasses tore through Boston at around 35 miles per hour, killing 21 people.
In the year 897 a pope had his dead predecessor dug up, dressed in robes, and put on trial. The corpse lost.
Young Julius Caesar was kidnapped by pirates. He joked he would crucify them once freed, then did exactly that.
To fix the calendar, Julius Caesar made 46 BC the longest year in history, stretching it to 445 days.
Cleopatra was not ethnically Egyptian. She descended from Ptolemy, a Macedonian Greek general who served Alexander the Great.
Alexander the Great never lost a battle in over a decade of constant warfare across three continents.
The Great Wall of China is not one wall but a network of many walls, built and rebuilt over some 2,000 years.
Farmers digging a well in 1974 discovered the Terracotta Army, about 8,000 clay soldiers with individually distinct faces.
China's first emperor swallowed mercury pills that his court believed would make him immortal. They likely killed him instead.
The world's first government issued paper money appeared in Song dynasty China in the eleventh century.
Decades before Columbus, the Chinese admiral Zheng He led enormous treasure fleets as far as the coast of East Africa.
Workers who built the pyramids of Giza were paid partly in beer, receiving rations of several liters a day.
Pharaoh Ramses the Second ruled Egypt for 66 years and fathered around 100 children.
Egyptians mummified animals by the millions, including cats, crocodiles, ibises, and even scarab beetles.
Tutankhamun was buried with a dagger forged from meteorite iron, metal that literally fell from the sky.
One decree carved in three scripts, the Rosetta Stone let scholars finally crack Egyptian hieroglyphs in 1822.
History's oldest known customer complaint is a Babylonian clay tablet from around 1750 BC, griping about poor quality copper.
Writing was invented independently at least three times, in Mesopotamia, China, and Mesoamerica.
Gobekli Tepe in Turkey is a monumental stone complex over 11,000 years old. It predates Stonehenge by some 6,000 years.
Some of Stonehenge's stones were hauled from Wales, roughly 250 kilometers away, without wheels or metal tools.
People have lived at Jericho on and off for more than 10,000 years, making it one of the oldest towns on Earth.
Otzi the Iceman was murdered in the Alps 5,300 years ago. His frozen body still carries 61 tattoos and the arrowhead that killed him.
Napoleon was not short. He stood around 170 centimeters, perfectly average for a Frenchman of his era.
During a staged hunt, Napoleon was once charged by hundreds of rabbits that swarmed him instead of fleeing.
Mount Tambora's eruption in 1815 dimmed the sun worldwide, causing the Year Without a Summer and crop failures on two continents.
Mary Shelley was only 18 when she dreamed up Frankenstein during the cold, gloomy summer of 1816.
Marie Antoinette never said let them eat cake. The line appeared in print when she was still a child in Austria.
The last known widow of an American Civil War veteran died in 2020, more than 150 years after the war ended.
John Adams and Thomas Jefferson both died on July 4, 1826, exactly 50 years after the Declaration of Independence.
George Washington never lived in the White House. It was finished only after his presidency ended.
The Wright brothers' entire first flight was shorter than the wingspan of a modern jumbo jet.
Just 66 years separate the Wright brothers' first powered flight and Neil Armstrong walking on the moon.
Orville Wright lived long enough to hear about Chuck Yeager breaking the sound barrier in 1947.
On Christmas 1914, enemy soldiers climbed out of their trenches on the Western Front to swap gifts, sing carols, and play football.
Tsutomu Yamaguchi survived the atomic bombing of Hiroshima, took a train home to Nagasaki, and survived that bombing too.
The influenza pandemic of 1918 killed more people than the First World War itself.
British propaganda claimed carrots gave pilots night vision. The story hid the real secret, airborne radar.
The eastern Roman Empire outlived the west by nearly a thousand years, falling in 1453, just 39 years before Columbus sailed.
Women fought as gladiators in ancient Rome until an emperor banned the practice around the year 200.
Ancient Pompeii had fast food. Dozens of counters called thermopolia served hot meals to Romans on the go.
Vesuvius buried Pompeii in the year 79, and the city lay forgotten underground for nearly 1,700 years.
Rome burned through four emperors in the single year 69, as generals seized and lost the throne in rapid succession.
In the year 193 the Praetorian Guard killed the emperor and auctioned the entire Roman Empire to the highest bidder.
Vikings never wore horned helmets. That image was invented for opera costumes in the 1800s.
Iceland's parliament, the Althing, first met in the year 930 and still operates today.
Mansa Musa of Mali gave away so much gold on his pilgrimage to Mecca that he crashed gold prices in Cairo for years.
Timbuktu was a medieval center of scholarship, home to universities and hundreds of thousands of manuscripts.
To attack Constantinople in 1453, the Ottomans dragged some 70 warships overland on greased logs, bypassing the harbor's defenses.
Magna Carta lasted barely ten weeks before the pope annulled it in 1215. Reissued later, it became legend.
A Japanese temple construction company called Kongo Gumi operated continuously for over 1,400 years, from 578 to 2006.
At the 1904 Olympic marathon, the first finisher had ridden in a car and the declared winner was dosed with strychnine and brandy.
Tug of war was a genuine Olympic event, contested at five games until 1920.
Marie Curie's notebooks are still radioactive. They sit in lead lined boxes, and readers must sign a waiver.
In 1888 Bertha Benz took her husband's invention on the first real road trip, buying fuel at pharmacies along the 100 kilometer route.
The oldest known musical instruments are bone flutes from Germany, carved over 40,000 years ago.
Chess began in India around the sixth century as a game called chaturanga, meaning four divisions of the army.
The Royal Game of Ur is about 4,500 years old, and its rules survive on a Babylonian clay tablet.
Archaeologists have dug up loaded dice in Roman ruins. Cheating at games is at least 2,000 years old.
The Antikythera mechanism, a geared Greek device over 2,000 years old, predicted eclipses like a mechanical computer.
Athenians could vote to exile a politician for ten years by scratching his name onto pottery shards, a practice that gave us the word ostracism.
Marcus Crassus, Rome's richest man, ran a private fire brigade that bought burning buildings cheap before dousing the flames.
Revolutionary France briefly used decimal time, with 10 hour days, 100 minute hours, and 100 second minutes.
The marathon's odd distance was fixed in 1908 so the race could start at Windsor Castle and finish in front of the royal box.
The pirate queen Ching Shih commanded tens of thousands of pirates, defeated imperial fleets, and retired with a full pardon.
Under a 1667 treaty the Dutch kept a tiny nutmeg island called Run, while England kept Manhattan. Both sides felt satisfied.
Alexander Graham Bell wanted people to answer the telephone with ahoy. Hello won out instead.
The word quarantine comes from Venice, where arriving ships had to wait forty days during plague outbreaks.
Cambridge sent students home during the plague of 1665. Isaac Newton, back on the family farm, laid the groundwork for calculus and gravity.
In Georgian England pineapples were so expensive that hosts rented them as party centerpieces instead of eating them.
